3.2.a.info:
Longest Prefix Match (LPM) is the router's first and most important rule for choosing a route. When a packet arrives, the router compares the destination IP against every route in the table and counts how many leading bits of the subnet mask match. The route with the most matching bits — the longest mask — wins. Specificity always wins over anything else.

The algorithm is deterministic: take the destination IP, AND it with each route's mask, and see if the result equals that route's network. Among all routes that match, pick the one with the highest prefix length. A /28 (28 matching bits) beats a /24 (24 matching bits), which beats a /16, which beats a /8, which beats the default route /0.

Worked example: The routing table has 10.0.0.0/8 via R2, 10.1.0.0/16 via R3, and 10.1.1.0/24 via R4. A packet for 10.1.1.50 matches all three (the destination falls inside every range), but /24 is the most specific. The router forwards to R4 — period. The /8 and /16 entries are ignored even though they're valid matches.

LPM runs in hardware via the CEF FIB on modern Cisco routers. The FIB is organized as a trie (prefix tree) where each bit of the destination IP indexes deeper into the tree, so LPM completes in a handful of CPU cycles per lookup — not a linear scan of every route. This is why CEF can forward millions of packets per second on a single line card.

Exam lens: When you see 'which route does the router use for destination X?', circle the prefix length of every matching route and pick the highest. Do not look at AD, do not look at metric, until after you've applied LPM. Roughly one in three Domain 3 questions hinges on this single rule.

hack.memory: LPM = 'most bits match wins.' Think mailing address: 'USA' matches billions, 'Illinois' narrows it, '123 Main St Chicago 60601' points to one front door. Zip code precision beats country precision every time. /32 > /28 > /24 > /16 > /8 > /0.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure a router with 4 overlapping static routes to the same destination range at different prefix lengths (/8, /16, /24, /28). Send a ping — only the /28 next-hop lights up in Simulation mode. Remove the /28 and re-ping — the /24 path activates. Repeat down the chain to /8. This stair-step demo cements LPM faster than any lecture.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 15 (Routing Fundamentals) demonstrates LPM with a 3-route example. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16 formalizes the algorithm. Expect 2-3 exam questions where LPM is the ONLY thing you need — mastering this single rule is the highest-ROI move in Domain 3.

3.2.a.info:
This is the #1 exam trap in routing: longest prefix match always runs before administrative distance. Students who've memorized the AD table sometimes jump straight to 'connected beats static beats OSPF beats RIP' and pick the wrong route. AD is only consulted when multiple routes tie on prefix length — never before.

The classic trap scenario: The routing table contains C 10.1.0.0/16 is directly connected, Gi0/0 (AD 0) AND R 10.1.1.0/24 via 192.168.1.2 (AD 120, learned from RIP). A packet arrives for 10.1.1.50. A rushed answer is 'connected always wins, AD 0.' Wrong. Both routes match, but /24 is more specific than /16 — the RIP route wins outright. Connected's AD 0 never gets compared.

Why this order exists: Specificity communicates intent. If someone injected a /24 into the network, they did so because they want traffic for that specific subnet to follow a specific path. Overriding that with a broader /16 connected route would defeat the entire point of injecting the /24. Longest prefix encodes 'this is the most precise instruction I have,' and precision beats trust.

Extreme case — a /32 from RIP beats a /24 from connected. If RIP advertises a host route 10.1.1.50/32 and a connected route covers 10.1.1.0/24, a packet to 10.1.1.50 follows the RIP /32. This enables niche designs like selectively redirecting one host to a scrubbing/inspection node without touching the rest of the subnet.

Exam drill: When you see the question 'which route wins?', always ask in this order: (1) Are the prefix lengths equal? If no → pick the longest. STOP. (2) If yes → compare AD. (3) If AD also equal → compare metric. Write 'P → A → M' next to the question before evaluating.

hack.memory: Prefix > AD > Metric. 'P' comes before 'A' in the alphabet AND in the routing hierarchy. A /32 from the worst protocol still beats a /0 from the best source. Specificity is king; trust is the tiebreaker; cost is the final judge.
hack.practice: Lab a router with an OSPF-learned /24 (AD 110) and a static /28 (AD 1) covering part of the same range. Ping an address inside the /28 — the static wins because it's more specific, not because of AD. Now ping an address in the /24 but OUTSIDE the /28 — OSPF wins. Same two routes, two different results — driven entirely by LPM.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 15 hammers this trap with multiple examples. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16 includes a section titled 'Longest Prefix Match Wins.' Boson ExSim and Pearson practice tests both include questions specifically designed to catch the 'jump to AD' mistake. If you remember one thing from this entire subtopic, remember: P before A.

3.2.a.info:
A /32 route (in IPv4) is the most specific route possible — it matches exactly one IP address. The subnet mask 255.255.255.255 requires all 32 bits to match, so the route only applies to a single host. A /32 will always win LPM against any broader route because no prefix can be longer than 32.

Where /32 routes come from naturally:

  • Local (L) routes — Every Cisco router installs a /32 local route for each of its own interface IPs. L 192.168.1.1/32 is directly connected, Gi0/0 lives beside the C 192.168.1.0/24 route. The L /32 catches traffic destined to the router itself and processes it locally instead of forwarding.
  • Loopback interfaces — Cisco treats interface loopback 0 with any mask as a /32 in the routing table by default (this is the 'host route' behavior of loopbacks).
  • OSPF on point-to-point links — When OSPF's network type is p2p, it may advertise /32 host routes for the neighbor's interface IP.
  • Manual host routes — Admins create /32 static routes to surgically override broader routes, redirect one server's traffic, or black-hole a malicious host.

Practical use case — surgical traffic steering: The 10.1.1.0/24 subnet normally flows through R2, but you need to send server 10.1.1.50's traffic through R3 for inspection. Configure ip route 10.1.1.50 255.255.255.255 [R3-next-hop]. The /32 wins LPM over the /24, so only that one host's traffic deviates. Every other device in the /24 continues through R2.

Black-hole (discard) routes: ip route 10.6.6.6 255.255.255.255 null0 drops all traffic to host 10.6.6.6. Because /32 wins LPM, this works even if broader routes to 10.6.6.0/24 exist. Common for blocking C2 servers, malicious IPs, or test endpoints.

IPv6 equivalent: A /128 route is the single-host equivalent in IPv6 — 128 bits all matching. Same LPM behavior.

hack.memory: /32 = single-host precision. 32 bits is the maximum possible prefix length for IPv4, so no route can be MORE specific. Use cases: local routes (router's own IPs), surgical override, black-hole (null0). IPv6 version is /128.
hack.practice: Lab this: on R1 configure 'ip route 8.8.8.8 255.255.255.255 null0' while also having a default route to an ISP. Ping 8.8.8.8 → fails (null0 drops it because /32 wins). Ping 8.8.4.4 → succeeds through the default route. Same router, two Google DNS IPs, two outcomes — all because of the /32 host route.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 (Static Routing) shows /32 static configuration. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15 covers local routes. Exam may ask 'what type of route is L 192.168.1.1/32?' — answer: local route, auto-created for the router's own interface IP. Also know: removing an interface's IP deletes both the C and L routes simultaneously.

3.2.a.info:
The default route is 0.0.0.0/0 in IPv4 (or ::/0 in IPv6) — zero matching bits required, so it matches every possible destination. Because no prefix can be shorter than /0, the default route is the least specific route in the table. It always loses LPM to any other matching route, which is why it's called the gateway of last resort.

How the 0.0.0.0/0 match works: Any destination IP ANDed with a /0 mask equals 0.0.0.0 — the match succeeds trivially. So any packet the router doesn't have a more specific route for will fall through to the default. Without a default route, those unknown-destination packets are dropped and the router sends an ICMP Destination Unreachable back to the source.

Typical placement: Enterprise edge routers and branch routers point a default route at the ISP (ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1). Core routers deep inside a large network may learn the default dynamically via OSPF (default-information originate) or BGP. Home routers always have a default route to the ISP; otherwise you couldn't browse anything beyond your LAN.

Reading the routing table: S* 0.0.0.0/0 [1/0] via 203.0.113.1 — the S is static, the * marks it as a candidate default (gateway of last resort), the [1/0] is [AD/metric]. At the top of show ip route output, you'll see Gateway of last resort is 203.0.113.1 to network 0.0.0.0. If you see Gateway of last resort is not set — there's no default and unknown destinations get dropped.

Why default routes shrink routing tables: The full internet routing table is ~900,000+ prefixes. A branch office doesn't need any of that — one default route pointing upstream covers every internet destination with a single entry. This is why enterprise edge designs use default routes instead of carrying the global BGP table: simplicity, smaller FIB, faster convergence.

nodes: Forward to gateway of last resort

hack.memory: Default route = 'when in doubt, ship it upstream.' /0 matches EVERY destination but loses LPM to any more specific route. It's the 'miscellaneous' drawer. The S* in 'show ip route' means 'static, candidate default.' No default = 'Gateway of last resort is not set' = unreachable destinations get dropped.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: on a branch router, remove all specific routes and keep only 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [ISP-next-hop]'. Ping any random internet IP (8.8.8.8, 1.1.1.1, 192.0.2.1) — all succeed through the default. Then 'no ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 ...' and watch every ping fail with 'Destination host unreachable.' Re-add and verify recovery.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 covers default route configuration. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Exam guarantee: at least one question either asks you to configure a default route or recognize S* in routing output as a candidate default. Know both IPv4 ('ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 ...') and IPv6 ('ipv6 route ::/0 ...') syntax cold.

3.2.a.info:
show ip route [destination-ip] is the ground-truth verification command for LPM. Instead of forcing you to eyeball the full routing table and manually determine which route wins, it returns the exact route the router will use for that specific IP. No guessing, no arithmetic — the router tells you its forwarding decision directly.

Example output:

R1# show ip route 10.1.1.50
Routing entry for 10.1.1.0/24
  Known via "ospf 1", distance 110, metric 20, type intra area
  Last update from 192.168.1.2 on GigabitEthernet0/0, 00:05:32 ago
  Routing Descriptor Blocks:
  * 192.168.1.2, from 2.2.2.2, 00:05:32 ago, via GigabitEthernet0/0
      Route metric is 20, traffic share count is 1

This output reveals: (1) the matched prefix (10.1.1.0/24 — the router chose this from potentially many overlapping prefixes), (2) the source protocol (OSPF 1), (3) AD (110), (4) metric (20), (5) next-hop and exit interface (192.168.1.2 via Gi0/0), (6) age of the route. The asterisk (*) next to the next-hop indicates the active traffic share in ECMP scenarios.

Compare with full output: show ip route dumps the entire RIB — dozens or hundreds of lines. You still have to apply LPM manually against the destination. show ip route 10.1.1.50 collapses that work into a one-line answer.

When to use it:

  • Verifying LPM: After configuring a more specific route, confirm the router actually uses it.
  • Troubleshooting black-hole or wrong-path issues: 'Traffic going the wrong way' usually means a more specific route is hijacking it — this command finds the culprit.
  • Debugging policy-based routing: Know the FIB's current best path before layering route-maps on top.
  • ECMP verification: If load-balancing is active, you'll see multiple Routing Descriptor Blocks in the output, each with its own next-hop.

Related helpful commands: show ip cef [destination] shows the CEF FIB entry (what actually forwards the packet in hardware); show ip route summary gives counts per protocol; traceroute [destination] confirms the real path end-to-end.

hack.memory: 'show ip route [IP]' = ask the router itself. Skip the arithmetic — the router already did the LPM. Reads like a confession: 'I'll use THIS route via THIS next-hop because of THIS source protocol.' Best single command for forwarding-decision verification.
hack.practice: Lab a router with 5 overlapping routes to 10.1.1.0/x at different prefix lengths. Before running the command, predict which route should win via LPM. Then 'show ip route 10.1.1.50' — verify the router agrees. Do this 10 times with different destination IPs inside and outside each prefix range. Your prediction accuracy teaches you LPM faster than any flashcard.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 11 and 15 demonstrate this command. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. This is the single most useful CLI command for verifying forwarding on the CCNA exam lab sims — expect to run it during troubleshooting questions. Also know: the output shows the PARENT prefix matched, not the destination IP itself, which confirms you understand LPM.

3.2.b.info:
Administrative Distance (AD) is the router's second decision criterion — used only when two or more routes share the exact same prefix length but were learned from different routing sources. The router picks the route with the lowest AD; the higher-AD route is kept by its originating protocol process but does not appear in the installed routing table.

Think of AD as a 'trust score.' The lower the number, the more the router trusts that source. Directly connected routes (AD 0) are maximally trusted because the router physically sees the interface. Static routes (AD 1) are trusted because a human explicitly typed them. Dynamic protocols get progressively higher AD based on Cisco's view of their sophistication: EIGRP (90) < OSPF (110) < RIP (120).

Worked example: R1 learns about 10.1.1.0/24 via both OSPF (AD 110) and RIP (AD 120). Both routes have the same /24 prefix length, so LPM cannot decide. The router compares AD: 110 < 120, so OSPF wins. show ip route 10.1.1.0 shows O 10.1.1.0/24 [110/20] via ... — the RIP version lives in RIP's internal database but doesn't populate the RIB.

Why AD is the tiebreaker and not metric: Metrics are protocol-specific and not comparable across protocols. OSPF cost (based on bandwidth) and RIP hop count (based on router count) measure completely different things — you can't meaningfully ask 'is OSPF cost 5 better than RIP cost 3?' AD sidesteps that by saying 'trust the more sophisticated protocol first, regardless of its metric.'

Common design pattern — primary + backup: Configure OSPF as the primary routing protocol AND a static route with AD > 110 to the same destination. The OSPF route (AD 110) wins while available. If OSPF fails (neighbor down, link failure), the static route (AD > 110) is no longer beaten and gets installed. Recovery is automatic.

AD is not consulted when prefix lengths differ. If RIP advertises 10.1.1.0/24 and connected has 10.1.0.0/16, LPM picks /24 without ever touching AD. The order is strict: Prefix → AD → Metric.

hack.memory: AD = trust score, triggered ONLY after prefix length ties. Two routes for 10.1.1.0/24 — one from OSPF (110), one from RIP (120)? OSPF wins. Connected=0 (eyewitness), Static=1 (boss said), OSPF=110 (reliable), RIP=120 (sketchy gossip). Lower = more trusted.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: enable both OSPF and RIP on R1, and configure both to learn the same 10.1.1.0/24 network. Run 'show ip route 10.1.1.0' — only OSPF (O) appears. Then 'show ip rip database' — RIP still knows the route, it just isn't installed. Shut the OSPF interface → within seconds, the RIP route takes over and appears in the table. Bring OSPF back up → RIP is evicted again.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24 (OSPF) and Day 11 (Static) establish AD. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Exam commonly asks 'the same route is learned via OSPF and RIP — which one is installed?' Answer: lower AD = OSPF. Always verify prefix lengths match first before invoking AD.

3.2.b.info:
When a route loses the AD tiebreaker, it does not disappear — it continues to live in its source protocol's internal database. The protocol keeps topology information, runs its algorithms, and maintains the losing route as a ready-to-install backup. If the winning route is ever removed (link down, neighbor lost, admin intervention), the losing route is immediately promoted and installed in the main routing table.

Per-prefix selection, not per-protocol selection: Only ONE route per prefix makes it into the routing table (barring ECMP). All other candidates are backups. This is why you see 'show ip ospf database' and 'show ip rip database' showing prefixes that are NOT in 'show ip route' — those protocols still know the routes, they just lost the AD comparison for now.

Example lifecycle:

  1. Initial state: OSPF and RIP both learn 10.1.1.0/24. OSPF (AD 110) wins, installed in RIB. RIP (AD 120) kept in RIP database as backup.
  2. OSPF neighbor goes down: The OSPF route is removed from the RIB.
  3. RIP is re-evaluated: With OSPF gone, RIP's route is the best available for /24. RIP installs it in the RIB — takeover happens within seconds.
  4. OSPF neighbor recovers: OSPF re-learns the route, AD 110 beats AD 120, OSPF is reinstalled, RIP returns to backup status.

Why this matters for resilience: You get automatic failover with no manual intervention. Redundant routing protocols (or static + dynamic combos) can provide near-zero-downtime failover — typically 10-30 seconds, depending on convergence timers.

Design pattern — floating static route: Configure a static route with AD higher than the dynamic protocol, e.g., ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.2.2 200. Normally the dynamic protocol (AD 110) wins, and the static (AD 200) sits dormant in the static database. If the dynamic protocol loses the route, the floating static becomes active. This is the most common way to implement deterministic backup paths without running a second dynamic protocol.

Verification commands: show ip route shows the active (winning) route. show ip ospf database, show ip rip database, show ip eigrp topology, and show ip bgp show routes known to those protocols — including backups. show running-config | include ip route shows configured static routes even if not currently active.

hack.memory: Losing the AD battle ≠ being deleted. Protocols keep their losers benched, ready to sub in if the starter gets injured. Two databases at play: the RIB (only winners) and per-protocol databases (everyone who knows the route). Floating static routes exploit this: AD 200 static sleeps until OSPF AD 110 disappears.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure OSPF + a floating static to the same 10.1.1.0/24. 'show ip route' shows OSPF winning. 'show running-config | include ip route' still shows the static config (alive but not active). Shut OSPF's exit interface — the static takes over within seconds. Unshut it — OSPF reclaims the slot. This is THE canonical failover pattern on the CCNA exam.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 demonstrates floating static routes. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15 covers the per-protocol database concept. Exam typically shows: 'OSPF route is active. OSPF goes down. Which route takes over?' — answer comes from the protocol database OR a floating static. Know both mechanisms.

3.2.b.info:
This rule closes the most common exam trap in Domain 3: administrative distance is NEVER used to compare routes with different prefix lengths. If prefix lengths differ, LPM decides without ever touching AD. Students who jump to the AD table lose these questions every single time.

The incorrect mental model: 'I know Connected has the lowest AD (0), so any connected route should beat a RIP route.' This is wrong whenever the RIP route is more specific. LPM runs first, in isolation, before AD is even considered.

Canonical trap: The routing table has C 10.0.0.0/8 is directly connected, Gi0/0 (AD 0) and R 10.1.1.0/24 via 192.168.2.2 (AD 120). Destination = 10.1.1.50. Both match. A student thinking 'AD 0 always wins' picks connected. The correct answer is RIP — the /24 is more specific than the /8, and LPM alone decides. AD never enters the comparison.

Why the rule is strict: The router evaluates routes in a hierarchy that mirrors what specificity communicates. A longer prefix says 'this is precise guidance' — it's the most detailed match available. A shorter prefix with a lower AD says 'I'm a trustworthy source giving broad guidance.' Precision beats trust when precision is available. Only when two routes are equally precise does the router ask 'which source do I trust more?'

Drill this with examples:

  • /24 RIP vs /16 Connected → RIP wins (LPM, AD irrelevant).
  • /32 RIP vs /24 Connected → RIP wins (LPM).
  • /24 OSPF vs /24 RIP → OSPF wins (same prefix, AD decides: 110 < 120).
  • /24 Connected vs /24 Static → Connected wins (same prefix, AD decides: 0 < 1).
  • /24 Static (AD 200) vs /24 OSPF (AD 110) → OSPF wins (same prefix, AD decides: floating static).

Fast self-check: Before comparing AD, write '/X vs /Y' for each candidate. If X ≠ Y, you've already finished — longer prefix wins. Only if X = Y do you proceed to AD.

children.name: Different prefix lengths? → LPM decides, STOP
Same prefix length? → Compare AD next

hack.memory: Different masks = LPM handles it alone, AD sits this one out. Same mask = AD enters the ring. 'P before A. ALWAYS.' Draw a little flowchart: 'Prefixes equal? No → pick longer. Yes → pick lower AD.' Ten seconds of doodling saves you every forwarding-decision question.
hack.practice: Write 10 mini-scenarios: each has 2-3 routes with mixed prefix lengths and AD sources. For each, circle the prefix length first. If distinct, pick the longest. If tied, compare AD. Do this until you can't mess it up. Then verify in Packet Tracer with 'show ip route [dest]' — the router's answer should match yours every time.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 15 addresses this trap explicitly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Boson and Pearson practice exams include several 'jump to AD' traps per test. Nailing this rule is worth an easy 3-5 points on the real exam. It's one of the highest-leverage pieces of understanding in Domain 3.

3.2.b.info:
show ip ospf database displays OSPF's Link State Database (LSDB) — the complete set of topology information OSPF has learned from its neighbors. This includes routes that may or may not be installed in the main routing table. It's how you verify what OSPF knows versus what it has installed.

OSPF is a link-state protocol: every router in an area maintains an identical copy of the LSDB, populated by Link State Advertisements (LSAs) flooded from all OSPF speakers. The SPF (Dijkstra) algorithm runs against the LSDB to compute best paths, and only those winners get installed in the RIB. Routes that lose to a static (AD 1) or another protocol with lower AD remain in OSPF's LSDB but don't appear in the RIB.

Typical output structure:

R1# show ip ospf database
            OSPF Router with ID (1.1.1.1) (Process ID 1)

        Router Link States (Area 0)
Link ID         ADV Router      Age         Seq#        Checksum Link count
1.1.1.1         1.1.1.1         120         0x80000003  0x00A2B4 2
2.2.2.2         2.2.2.2         118         0x80000005  0x00C3D5 2

        Net Link States (Area 0)
Link ID         ADV Router      Age         Seq#        Checksum
192.168.1.1     1.1.1.1         120         0x80000001  0x00E8F6

Why this matters for forwarding decisions: If someone asks 'why isn't OSPF installing this route?', the answer usually comes from comparing show ip ospf database (what OSPF knows) to show ip route ospf (what OSPF installed). Missing in the RIB but present in the LSDB? Most likely a better AD source (static, connected, EIGRP) is winning the prefix.

Useful variants: show ip ospf database router (Type 1 LSAs — per-router topology), show ip ospf database network (Type 2 — multi-access segments), show ip ospf database summary (Type 3 — inter-area prefixes), show ip ospf database external (Type 5 — redistributed external routes). show ip ospf neighbor is the companion command for verifying adjacencies.

On the CCNA: You won't be asked to decode LSA types in depth, but you should recognize that the OSPF database is separate from the routing table and that 'missing from routing table' ≠ 'OSPF doesn't know.'

right.label: RIB (installed)
items: Only routes that beat other sources' AD

hack.memory: 'show ip ospf database' = what OSPF KNOWS. 'show ip route ospf' = what OSPF INSTALLED. The first is a superset of the second. If a route is in the LSDB but not the RIB, something with lower AD (static, connected, EIGRP) grabbed that prefix first.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: build a 3-router OSPF topology in Area 0. Run 'show ip ospf database' on each — all three should have identical LSDBs (that's the link-state guarantee). Now add a static route on R1 to one of the OSPF networks. 'show ip route' on R1 shows the static (S, AD 1). 'show ip ospf database' still shows OSPF knows the network. OSPF is aware, just outranked.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 24-26 cover OSPF database concepts lightly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 19-21 detail LSA types. The CCNA won't require deep LSA-type knowledge, but recognizing 'database vs routing table' is fair game. This is bridge knowledge toward CCNP ENCOR.

3.2.c.info:
The default administrative distance values must be memorized for the CCNA exam. These are Cisco IOS conventions (not RFC standards), and they determine which routing source wins when multiple sources advertise the same prefix with equal prefix lengths.

Complete table (memorize all 10):

  • Directly Connected: 0 — Physical interface, highest trust.
  • Static: 1 — Admin-configured, deliberate.
  • eBGP: 20 — External BGP, from another autonomous system.
  • EIGRP (internal): 90 — Cisco's proprietary IGP, fast convergence via DUAL.
  • OSPF: 110 — Open standard IGP, link-state. All OSPF route types (intra-area, inter-area, E1, E2) share this AD.
  • IS-IS: 115 — ISP/large enterprise link-state, rarely on CCNA.
  • RIP: 120 — Distance-vector, hop count metric, legacy.
  • EIGRP (external): 170 — Routes redistributed into EIGRP from another protocol. Higher than OSPF because redistributed routes are less trusted than natively learned ones.
  • iBGP: 200 — Internal BGP, routes from within the same AS. Lower priority than IGPs because iBGP often carries distant prefixes learned from external BGP.
  • Unknown / Unusable: 255 — The route is never installed. Setting AD to 255 effectively disables a route.

Why eBGP is lower than IGPs (AD 20): Counterintuitive, but the logic is that eBGP carries routes from external networks you don't control, and the router should trust external BGP advertisements over internal rumor. iBGP, by contrast, often echoes eBGP-learned routes across the internal network, so it's ranked low (AD 200) to prevent iBGP from winning over natively-learned IGP routes within the same AS.

Why EIGRP internal (90) beats OSPF (110): Cisco's internal view — both protocols are reliable, but EIGRP's DUAL algorithm is faster and more efficient in Cisco's eyes. In mixed environments where EIGRP and OSPF cover the same prefixes, EIGRP wins by default. You can override with distance N under the protocol config.

hack.memory: CHANT: 'Zero-one-twenty, ninety-one-ten, one-fifteen-one-twenty, one-seventy, two hundred, two fifty-five.' Mnemonic: C=0, S=1, E=20, E=90, O=110, I=115, R=120 — the first letters spell 'CSEEOIR.' Use: 'Can Someone Explain EIGRP/OSPF/IS-IS/RIP?' Then tack on: EIGRP-ext 170, iBGP 200, Unusable 255.
hack.practice: Make 10 flashcards: protocol on one side, AD value on the other. Shuffle. Drill both directions — given protocol → state AD, given AD → state protocol. Keep at it until you can recite the entire table in under 15 seconds. Then recite it backwards (255 → 200 → 170 → ...). If you stumble on any value, that's the one to drill hardest.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 presents the AD table early. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15 has the authoritative list. GUARANTEED on the exam — multiple questions rely on knowing these values. Write the full table on your scratch whiteboard in the first 2 minutes of the exam before you touch any question. It's worth easy points.

3.2.c.info:
Brute-force repetition is the only reliable way to internalize the AD values. You don't need to derive them, understand them deeply, or calculate them — just recall them instantly. The chant '0, 1, 20, 90, 110, 115, 120, 170, 200' covers everything you'll be asked.

Why chanting works: Your brain stores ordered sequences (phone numbers, song lyrics, number pi to 10 digits) much better than isolated facts. By linking the AD values in strict ascending order, you create a single recall chain instead of 10 separate memories. Pulling any one value drags the rest along with it.

Daily drill (5 minutes):

  1. Say aloud, ascending: 'Connected zero, Static one, eBGP twenty, EIGRP ninety, OSPF one-ten, IS-IS one-fifteen, RIP one-twenty, EIGRP-ext one-seventy, iBGP two-hundred.' Three times in a row.
  2. Say aloud, descending: Reverse the chain. Forces retrieval, not just recognition.
  3. Random direction: Quiz yourself — 'AD 110?' → 'OSPF.' 'EIGRP internal?' → '90.' Mix both directions.
  4. Time trial: Stopwatch. Full table in under 15 seconds. Then under 10. Then under 7.

Exam whiteboard strategy: During the exam tutorial (the first 2 minutes before questions start), transfer the AD table onto your scratch whiteboard. Also jot: 'PAM = Prefix → AD → Metric,' 'LPM wins first,' 'ECMP default 4 paths,' and any formulas you've drilled. This 'brain dump' frees working memory — you don't have to hold these facts under exam pressure, you just glance at the whiteboard.

Make the chant spatial: Physically write the table from memory, top to bottom, every morning for a week. Motor memory reinforces verbal memory. By test day the table writes itself.

hack.memory: Chant daily: '0-1-20-90-110-115-120-170-200.' 9 numbers, 9 protocols. Like memorizing a phone number with meaning. Time trial: full recall under 10 seconds. Whiteboard brain-dump at the start of the exam — transfer everything from memory to paper in 2 minutes before questions start.
hack.practice: Morning ritual, one week straight: (1) Write the AD table from scratch on paper. (2) Chant it aloud 3 times. (3) Reverse recall — given AD 170, name the protocol. By day 3 it's automatic. By day 7 you could recite it during a fire drill. The exam can't trip you up on something you've recited 50 times.
hack.meta: Not a content fact — this is a meta-study technique. Jeremy mentions the chant approach in Day 11. The trick is consistency: 5 minutes daily crushes 2 hours of frantic review the night before. Build the habit now, not the day before the exam.

3.2.c.info:
The default AD values are not immutable — Cisco IOS lets you override them at the protocol level or per-route. This is how floating static routes, route preference tuning, and selective backup designs work.

Per-protocol modification (global): Under a routing protocol's configuration, use the distance command to change its AD for all routes it learns.

R1(config)# router ospf 1
R1(config-router)# distance 130

After this, OSPF routes have AD 130 instead of 110. If EIGRP is also running (AD 90), EIGRP continues to win over OSPF, but now RIP (AD 120) would beat OSPF. This is rare in production but useful in specific redistribution scenarios to break loops or enforce preferences.

Per-route modification (static routes): Append an AD value to the ip route command to create a floating static route.

R1(config)# ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.2.2 210

The 210 at the end sets this static's AD to 210. Normally static routes have AD 1, but this one has 210 — so it's beaten by OSPF (110), EIGRP (90), RIP (120), and anything else with lower AD. It only gets installed if no other route exists for this prefix. Perfect backup path.

EIGRP-specific per-route AD: distance eigrp [internal-AD] [external-AD] lets you modify EIGRP's internal and external AD separately.

OSPF / RIP per-source AD: distance [AD] [source-IP] [wildcard-mask] under the routing protocol lets you adjust AD for routes from a specific neighbor. Rarely needed on CCNA.

Verification: show ip protocols displays the current AD for each running routing protocol. show ip route shows per-route AD in the [AD/metric] brackets. After changing AD, always verify the routing table reflects the change — sometimes clear-and-re-learn is needed.

Exam relevance: The floating static syntax (static route + AD number at end) is the most likely thing you'll see. Know the default AD values so you can pick a higher AD than the dynamic protocol you're backing up. The 'distance' command under OSPF/EIGRP is lower priority on CCNA but appears occasionally in sim questions.

nodes: New AD takes effect after clear

hack.memory: Two ways to modify AD: 'distance N' under a routing protocol (changes AD for all that protocol's routes) OR tack an AD number onto the end of a static route ('ip route ... 210'). Floating static = static with AD HIGHER than the dynamic protocol it's backing up.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure OSPF + a floating static with AD 210 to the same 10.1.1.0/24. 'show ip route 10.1.1.0' shows OSPF (O, AD 110). 'show run | include ip route' shows the static is configured but dormant. Shut the OSPF interface — static takes over (S, AD 210 in brackets). Unshut OSPF — static is evicted. This is the canonical CCNA failover lab.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 demonstrates floating static syntax. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. The exam lab sim may ask you to configure a floating static as a backup to an OSPF route — practice the exact syntax until it's muscle memory: 'ip route [dest] [mask] [next-hop] [AD].' Forgetting the AD number defaults to 1 and your 'backup' becomes the primary.

3.2.d.info:
Metric is the router's third and final decision criterion. It's consulted only when two or more routes tie on BOTH prefix length AND administrative distance — which means they came from the same routing protocol. The route with the lowest metric wins; if metrics are also tied, the router performs ECMP load balancing.

Each routing protocol defines its metric differently:

  • OSPF — Cost: Reference bandwidth (default 100 Mbps) divided by interface bandwidth. A 1 Gbps interface has cost 1, a 100 Mbps interface has cost 1, a 10 Mbps interface has cost 10. Path cost = sum of outgoing interface costs along the path. Modify with auto-cost reference-bandwidth [Mbps] for modern high-speed links.
  • RIP — Hop Count: The number of routers between here and the destination. Max 15; 16 means 'unreachable.' Simple but ignores link speed — a 1 Gbps 3-hop path loses to a 10 Mbps 2-hop path.
  • EIGRP — Composite Metric: A weighted formula based on minimum bandwidth and cumulative delay (defaults: K1=K3=1, K2=K4=K5=0). The result is a large integer in the millions. Much more granular than OSPF cost.
  • BGP — Path Attributes: Not a single metric; BGP evaluates a cascade of attributes (AS-path length, local preference, MED, origin type, etc.) instead of a numeric metric.

Worked example (OSPF): Two OSPF routes to 10.1.1.0/24. Path A: three Gi links → cost 1+1+1 = 3. Path B: one Gi + one FastEthernet (cost 10) → cost 1+10 = 11. Both are /24 (LPM tie), both are OSPF (AD tie), so metric decides: 3 < 11, Path A wins.

Metric comparison is protocol-scoped: You cannot compare OSPF cost to RIP hop count directly — they measure different things. That's why AD (not metric) is the tiebreaker across protocols. Metric only enters the picture within a single protocol.

Verification: show ip route [destination] shows the metric in the brackets [AD/metric]. show ip ospf interface shows per-interface cost. show ip eigrp topology shows EIGRP's composite metrics.

hack.memory: Metric = final tiebreaker, same protocol only. OSPF: lower cost wins. RIP: fewer hops. EIGRP: lower composite. Protocols can't cross-compare metrics — apples and oranges. If prefix + AD + metric all tie → ECMP load balancing kicks in.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: build a diamond topology (R1 → R2 → R4, R1 → R3 → R4). All routers run OSPF. Make the R1-R2-R4 path use Gi interfaces (cost 1+1=2) and the R1-R3-R4 path use FastEthernet (cost 10+10=20). 'show ip route' on R1 shows only the cost-2 path. Change one Gi to FastEthernet — costs become 1+10=11 vs 10+10=20 — still the first path wins. Make them both cost 20 — ECMP kicks in (both paths install).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 24-26 (OSPF) cover cost calculation explicitly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam commonly shows OSPF cost math: '1 Gbps + 100 Mbps + 1 Gbps path, what's the cost?' = 1+1+1 = 3 (using default 100 Mbps ref-bw, a 100 Mbps interface still rounds to 1). Memorize: ref-bw / interface-bw, minimum 1.

3.2.d.info:
Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP) is what happens when the router finds multiple routes that tie on prefix length, AD, AND metric — it installs ALL of them in the routing table and distributes traffic across them. This gives you automatic load balancing without any explicit configuration, and automatic failover if one path goes down (the other paths keep carrying traffic).

Default ECMP path counts:

  • OSPF: Default 4 paths, max 32 (maximum-paths 1-32 under router ospf).
  • EIGRP: Default 4 paths, max 32. EIGRP also supports unequal-cost load balancing via the variance command — unique to EIGRP among the major CCNA protocols.
  • RIP: Default 4 paths, max 16.
  • Static: Multiple static routes with the same prefix/mask/AD → all installed → ECMP.
  • BGP: ECMP disabled by default; enable with maximum-paths under router bgp.

Routing table appearance: An ECMP route shows multiple next-hops for one prefix:

O  10.1.1.0/24 [110/20] via 192.168.1.2, 00:02:14, GigabitEthernet0/0
             [110/20] via 192.168.2.2, 00:02:14, GigabitEthernet0/1

One prefix, two routing-descriptor blocks, same AD and metric. CEF hashes traffic across both next-hops.

Why 4 paths by default: Historical balance between hardware cost (more paths = more FIB memory) and typical redundancy needs (most topologies have 2-4 equal-cost paths). Modern networks with spine-leaf fabrics often bump this to 16 or 32 for ECMP-heavy load sharing.

Load balancing behavior (CEF): Default is per-destination load balancing using a source-destination IP hash. All packets in a given flow follow the same path (preventing out-of-order delivery). Different flows may be hashed to different paths, statistically balancing traffic. Alternative: per-packet load balancing (round-robin), which balances perfectly but can reorder packets and damage TCP performance — rarely used.

Failover: If one ECMP path goes down, CEF removes it from the FIB and continues hashing across the remaining paths. No routing re-convergence needed for the surviving paths — sub-second failover.

hack.memory: ECMP = Equal Cost Multi-Path. Tied on prefix + AD + metric? Install all, load-balance across them. Default = 4 paths, max = 32 (OSPF/EIGRP/RIP). Change with 'maximum-paths N' under the protocol. EIGRP alone supports unequal-cost LB via 'variance.'
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: create a 4-path OSPF topology from R1 to R5's LAN — all four paths traverse Gi links with identical cost. 'show ip route 10.1.1.0' shows one prefix, four next-hop lines (ECMP active). Set 'maximum-paths 2' under 'router ospf 1' — now only 2 paths install, the others are backups. Set back to 4 — all four return.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25 (OSPF) covers ECMP. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam commonly asks 'how many paths appear in the routing table when all are equal cost?' — answer: up to 'maximum-paths' value (default 4). Know the number 4 cold. Also expect: 'what command changes the path count?' = maximum-paths.

3.2.d.info:
The maximum-paths command controls how many equal-cost paths a routing protocol will install in the routing table for a single prefix. It directly caps ECMP load balancing on a per-protocol basis.

Syntax:

R1(config)# router ospf 1
R1(config-router)# maximum-paths 8

This sets OSPF to install up to 8 equal-cost paths per prefix. Same syntax applies under router eigrp, router rip, and router bgp configurations.

Default and range:

  • OSPF: default 4, range 1-32.
  • EIGRP: default 4, range 1-32.
  • RIP: default 4, range 1-16.
  • BGP: default 1 (ECMP disabled), range 1-32.

Setting maximum-paths 1 disables ECMP — even if multiple paths are equal-cost, only one gets installed (the first one selected by the protocol's internal tiebreaker, like lowest next-hop router ID).

When to increase above 4:

  • Spine-leaf data center fabrics — A leaf switch may have 8-32 equal-cost paths to a spine. Set maximum-paths 32 to use all of them.
  • High-throughput WAN designs with multiple equal-cost links to the same remote site.
  • Redundant core uplinks where all uplinks are provisioned for ECMP load sharing.

When to decrease:

  • Hardware constraints — Older platforms may not support deep ECMP in the FIB. Lower max-paths if you see FIB exhaustion errors.
  • Application sensitivity — Some stateful applications break when their flows are hashed across different paths (rare with per-destination LB, but possible with per-packet).
  • Troubleshooting — Temporarily set max-paths 1 to isolate which path carries problematic traffic.

Verification: show ip protocols displays the current max-paths setting for each protocol. show ip route shows the actual installed paths per prefix.

CCNA exam angle: Know the default (4) and the command syntax. Sim questions may ask you to enable 8 ECMP paths under OSPF — answer: router ospf 1 → maximum-paths 8.

left.label: Default: maximum-paths 4
items: Up to 4 ECMP paths per prefix

right.label: Tuned: maximum-paths 32
items: Up to 32 ECMP paths per prefix

hack.memory: 'maximum-paths N' under the routing protocol config. Default 4 for OSPF/EIGRP/RIP. Max 32 (OSPF/EIGRP) or 16 (RIP). BGP default is 1 (ECMP OFF) — must enable explicitly. Lower to 1 to disable ECMP.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure an OSPF topology with 6 equal-cost paths from R1 to a destination. Default behavior — only 4 install. Run 'maximum-paths 6' under router ospf 1 — all 6 appear. Set to 1 — only one shows. Set to 32 — all 6 stay (you asked for more than exist, router uses what's available).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25 mentions maximum-paths in OSPF context. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam may ask default values (4) or config syntax. Not a heavy-hitter topic but worth knowing for lab sims and a recall question or two.

3.2.d.info:
Cisco Express Forwarding (CEF) is the default switching mode on modern Cisco routers, and when it handles ECMP, it uses per-destination load balancing by default. This means all packets belonging to a single source-destination pair follow the same ECMP path, preserving flow affinity and avoiding out-of-order delivery.

The hashing algorithm: CEF takes the source and destination IP addresses, runs them through a hash function, and uses the result modulo the number of ECMP paths to select which path to use. Same source-dest pair → same hash → same path every time. Different flows → potentially different paths → statistical load distribution.

Why per-destination is the default:

  • TCP hates reordering. TCP flows that get packets in the wrong order treat it as packet loss, trigger fast retransmit, and throttle themselves. Per-destination LB keeps TCP happy.
  • Stateful firewalls and NAT. Asymmetric routing (packets going one way, return going another) breaks stateful inspection. Per-destination keeps both directions on the same path.
  • Simplicity of troubleshooting. Flow-level debugging is predictable: capture on one path, you see the whole flow.

Load balancing quality: With many distinct flows (typical in modern networks), per-destination LB distributes reasonably evenly. With very few flows (e.g., two big elephant flows between the same endpoints), all traffic can hash onto one path — poor balance. This is 'polarization' and can be mitigated with more granular hashing (L4 ports, MPLS labels) or by increasing ECMP width.

Alternative: per-packet LB. Configurable with ip load-sharing per-packet on the egress interface. Round-robins individual packets across ECMP paths. Perfect balance, but causes reordering — dangerous for TCP. Almost never used in production. Know it exists; don't deploy it.

Verification: show ip cef [dest-prefix] detail shows the CEF FIB entry and which paths it will hash across. show ip cef exact-route [src] [dst] predicts which specific path a flow will take — useful for debugging.

CCNA lens: Know CEF is default on modern Cisco gear, know per-destination is the default LB mode, know the reason (avoids reordering). Deep FIB internals are CCNP territory.

hack.memory: CEF per-destination LB = 'same flow, same path.' Hashes src+dst IPs to pick one of the ECMP paths — flow sticks to that path. Prevents TCP reordering and keeps stateful firewalls happy. Per-packet LB exists but will wreck TCP — avoid it.
hack.practice: On a real Cisco router with ECMP: 'show ip cef 10.1.1.0' shows the FIB entry. 'show ip cef exact-route 192.168.1.10 10.1.1.50' predicts which next-hop a flow between those two hosts will take. Run it with multiple source IPs — different hashes, different paths. Same source + dest pair → always the same path.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 8 briefly mentions CEF. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. The CCNA may ask 'what's the default load balancing mode for CEF?' = per-destination. Or 'why does CEF hash flows?' = avoid out-of-order delivery and preserve stateful affinity. Shallow but recallable.

3.2.e.info:
PAM = Prefix → AD → Metric. This 3-step hierarchy is THE algorithm every Cisco router uses to pick a single route from a table full of candidates. It's strictly ordered — each step is only reached if the previous step produced a tie. Skip a step or invert the order, and you'll get the answer wrong on the exam and see traffic go the wrong way in the lab.

Step 1 — Prefix (Longest Prefix Match): Find all routes whose network/mask matches the destination. Among those, pick the ones with the longest prefix length (most specific). If exactly one route has the longest prefix, the decision is final — install it, forward traffic, done.

Step 2 — AD (Administrative Distance): If multiple routes tie on prefix length, compare their AD values. The route with the lowest AD wins. AD is a trust score: Connected (0) beats Static (1) beats OSPF (110) beats RIP (120). If exactly one route has the lowest AD at this point, it's installed.

Step 3 — Metric: If routes also tie on AD (meaning they came from the same protocol), compare their metrics. The route with the lowest metric wins. If metrics are also tied, the router installs ALL tied routes as ECMP paths.

Why this specific order: Specificity communicates precise intent (Step 1). Trust communicates source reliability (Step 2). Cost communicates path quality within a single protocol's worldview (Step 3). Each step resolves a progressively more ambiguous situation — and each step is only meaningful once the previous one has run.

Common CCNA exam question pattern: 'Given this routing table, which route does the router use for destination X?' Walk through PAM methodically: (1) Circle every route whose network matches the destination. (2) Of those, mark the one(s) with the longest prefix. (3) If tied, pick lowest AD. (4) If still tied, pick lowest metric. Write 'P → A → M' in the margin before answering. This one habit catches every trap the exam throws at you.

hack.memory: PAM = Prefix, AD, Metric. 'Please Ask Mom.' Or 'Pick A Match.' Strictly ordered: Step 1 first, Step 2 only if tied, Step 3 only if still tied. Most CCNA forwarding questions resolve at Step 1 alone — don't overthink.
hack.practice: Find 20 practice-exam forwarding-decision questions. Before picking an answer, write 'P → A → M' next to the question. Apply each step in order. Check your answer against the key. The goal isn't just getting answers right — it's internalizing the ORDER so it's automatic by exam day. Target: 20/20 accuracy, under 45 seconds per question.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 11 and 15 walk through PAM examples. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16 formalizes the three-step hierarchy as 'the forwarding decision algorithm.' Every Domain 3 forwarding question derives from PAM. If you internalize nothing else in this topic, internalize PAM.

3.2.e.info:
Step 1 — Longest Prefix Match (LPM). This is always the first gate. The router compares the destination IP against every route in the RIB, keeps only the ones whose network/mask match, and of those, selects the route with the longest mask (highest prefix length).

LPM has absolute priority over everything else. A /32 from RIP (AD 120, the highest common IGP AD) beats a /24 from Connected (AD 0, the lowest possible AD). A /28 static (AD 1) is overridden by a /29 OSPF route (AD 110) because /29 is more specific than /28. The source of the route, its metric, its age — all irrelevant at Step 1. Only the prefix length matters.

Mechanics: For each route in the table, compute destination IP AND route-mask and check if it equals the route's network. Among all matches, pick the highest prefix length. Modern hardware does this in nanoseconds via a trie-based FIB lookup.

Why LPM is Step 1 and not Step 2: Specificity communicates the strongest possible intent. If someone has injected a /28 route into the network, they did so because traffic for that /28 needs to follow a specific path. Letting a broader /24 route (even from a trusted source) override that /28 would invalidate the /28's entire purpose. Network design depends on LPM running first.

Common exam trap — the 'bigger is better' illusion:

C 10.0.0.0/8 is directly connected, Gi0/0     (AD 0)
R 10.1.1.0/24 via 192.168.1.2                  (AD 120)
Dst: 10.1.1.50 → which route wins?

Trap answer: 'Connected, AD 0 is lowest.' Correct answer: 'RIP /24, because /24 > /8 at LPM.' The /24 wins before AD is even considered.

Verification: show ip route [destination-IP] returns the exact route LPM selected. Use this whenever you're unsure — the router does the math for you.

Edge case — equal prefix lengths: If multiple routes share the same longest prefix, LPM cannot decide alone. Step 2 (AD) takes over. This is the most common trigger for Step 2 in real networks: two protocols advertising the same /24, same prefix length, different AD.

hack.memory: Step 1 = prefix length ONLY. Longest mask wins. AD and metric don't exist yet at this step. /32 > /28 > /24 > /16 > /8 > /0. The exam loves trapping you with a low-AD broad route vs. a high-AD specific route — specific always wins.
hack.practice: Drill 10 prefix-length comparisons on paper. For each, circle the longest. Time yourself: 3 comparisons per 10 seconds. Once LPM is automatic, you can spot Step 1 winners instantly and skip all the AD/metric noise the exam throws at you.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 15 drills LPM. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16 explicitly warns about the 'jump to AD' trap. This concept shows up in ~3 exam questions, all with the same trap structure. Get it right once and you'll spot the pattern every time.

3.2.e.info:
Step 2 — Lowest AD. Only reached when Step 1 (LPM) produces a tie — multiple routes have the same longest prefix length. The router then compares their administrative distance values and picks the one with the lowest AD. The winner is installed in the RIB; losing routes go back to their source protocols' databases as backups.

Reference the AD table (from 3.2.c): Connected 0 → Static 1 → eBGP 20 → EIGRP 90 → OSPF 110 → IS-IS 115 → RIP 120 → EIGRP-ext 170 → iBGP 200 → Unknown 255. Lower = more trusted = wins.

Worked example: Routing table contains:

O  10.1.1.0/24 [110/20] via 192.168.1.2, GigabitEthernet0/0
R  10.1.1.0/24 [120/2] via 192.168.2.2, GigabitEthernet0/1

Both have prefix /24 (Step 1 tie). Step 2: OSPF (AD 110) vs RIP (AD 120). 110 < 120 → OSPF wins. Only the OSPF route appears in 'show ip route.'

When Step 2 is NOT reached: If Step 1 produces a single winner (one route with the longest prefix), AD is never consulted. This is the #1 trap — students jump to AD comparison without confirming prefix lengths match.

Same protocol → Step 2 will always tie: Two OSPF routes will both have AD 110 (same protocol, same AD). Step 2 declares a tie and passes to Step 3 (metric). Two RIP routes both have AD 120 — Step 3 again. Two static routes both have AD 1 — Step 3 again (if no floating AD overrides). The only way to avoid Step 3 is for the tied routes to come from different protocols with different default ADs.

Floating static routes work via Step 2: Configure a static with ip route ... 210 (AD 210). OSPF learns the same /24 with AD 110. At Step 2, OSPF (110) beats floating static (210). If OSPF disappears, the floating static has no competition and is installed. Entire failover mechanism runs through Step 2.

Key caveat: AD is Cisco-specific. Other vendors may use different default values or different terms ('route preference' in Junos). On CCNA, always assume Cisco defaults.

hack.memory: Step 2 = lowest AD wins, ONLY after prefix length tied. Same prefix + different protocols → AD decides. OSPF 110 beats RIP 120. Static 1 beats OSPF 110. Connected 0 beats all. Floating static (AD 210) beats nothing unless everything else dies.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure both OSPF and RIP to learn 10.1.1.0/24. Verify OSPF wins in 'show ip route.' Add a static with 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 [next-hop] 5' (AD 5) — static now beats OSPF. Add another static with AD 200 — OSPF still wins (110 < 200). Understanding each Step 2 comparison prevents every AD-related exam trap.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 11 and 15 demonstrate Step 2. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16. Exam questions on Step 2 are extremely common — often paired with Step 1 traps. Memorize the AD table from 3.2.c cold; Step 2 depends entirely on recall.

3.2.e.info:
Step 3 — Lowest Metric. Only reached when Steps 1 AND 2 both tie — meaning two or more routes share the same prefix length AND the same administrative distance, which means they were learned by the same routing protocol. The router then compares their protocol-specific metrics and picks the lowest. If metrics are also tied, ECMP load balancing kicks in.

Metric by protocol (recap):

  • OSPF = cost (reference_bandwidth / interface_bandwidth, summed across outgoing interfaces).
  • EIGRP = composite metric (bandwidth + delay, with K-values).
  • RIP = hop count (1-15, 16 = unreachable).
  • BGP = path attributes cascade, not a single metric.

Example (OSPF two-path): Two OSPF routes to 10.1.1.0/24, both AD 110, prefix /24.

O  10.1.1.0/24 [110/10] via 192.168.1.2, Gi0/0
O  10.1.1.0/24 [110/30] via 192.168.2.2, Gi0/1

Step 1: tied at /24. Step 2: tied at AD 110. Step 3: cost 10 < cost 30 → first route wins.

Metric tie → ECMP: If both paths had cost 10 (identical), BOTH would install with ECMP load balancing. 'show ip route' would show one prefix with two next-hop lines. CEF then hashes traffic across both.

Why metric is scoped to a single protocol: Metrics measure different things in different protocols. OSPF cost 20 means 'traversed enough low-bandwidth links to sum to 20' (relative to reference bandwidth). RIP hop count 5 means 'five routers away.' You cannot compare these numerically — they're incomparable. AD (Step 2) sidesteps this by saying 'we pick the protocol first based on trust, then let that protocol's metric decide within its own worldview.'

When Step 3 dominates planning: Network designers tune metrics (OSPF cost, EIGRP bandwidth/delay) to steer traffic along preferred paths without changing the logical topology. Lower the cost on a preferred uplink → OSPF prefers it at Step 3. Increase the cost on a congested link → OSPF deprefers it. This is the knob engineers turn for traffic engineering on enterprise networks.

EIGRP unequal-cost LB via 'variance': EIGRP alone supports unequal-cost ECMP — with variance N under the router, EIGRP installs feasible successor paths whose metric is within N times the best metric. Unique to EIGRP, commonly asked on exams.

hack.memory: Step 3 = same protocol, lowest metric wins. Tied metric → ECMP installs all. OSPF metric = cost. RIP metric = hops. EIGRP = composite. Protocols can't cross-compare metrics — Step 2 (AD) must tie first for Step 3 to ever run.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: build two equal-cost OSPF paths to 10.1.1.0/24 — verify ECMP in routing table (both paths install). Change one interface cost to 30 with 'ip ospf cost 30' — re-run 'show ip route,' only the low-cost path remains. Change it back — ECMP returns. Step 3 + tied metric = ECMP is the pattern CCNA loves to test.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 24-26 (OSPF) cover cost-based Step 3 decisions. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 20. Exam asks both 'which route wins on metric?' (simple comparison) and 'how many paths install when metrics are equal?' (ECMP, default 4). Drill both cases.

3.2.e.info:
The #1 forwarding-decision trap on the CCNA exam: skipping Step 1 (LPM) and jumping straight to Step 2 (AD) because the AD table is what students memorized hardest. This trap is embedded in nearly every Boson/Pearson/real-exam question that tests route selection with mixed sources.

The trap structure: Present the student with a routing table containing one very-specific route from a high-AD protocol (e.g., /28 from RIP, AD 120) and one broader route from a low-AD source (e.g., /24 from Connected, AD 0). Ask which route the router uses for a destination inside the /28.

Wrong (but tempting) reasoning: 'Connected has AD 0, the lowest possible AD. It wins. Answer: connected route.' This is how 30-40% of students pick — and they're wrong.

Correct reasoning: 'Step 1 first — LPM. /28 > /24. The /28 from RIP wins outright. AD is never compared. Answer: RIP route.'

Variants of the trap:

  • /32 RIP vs /24 Connected: /32 wins (LPM).
  • /28 Static (AD 210, floating) vs /24 OSPF (AD 110): Static /28 wins (LPM, not AD).
  • /16 OSPF vs /24 RIP: RIP /24 wins (LPM).
  • /24 EIGRP internal (AD 90) vs /24 RIP (AD 120): EIGRP wins at Step 2 (same prefix → AD decides).
  • /24 OSPF (cost 10) vs /24 OSPF (cost 20): Lower-cost OSPF wins at Step 3 (same prefix, same AD → metric decides).

Mental checklist for every forwarding question:

  1. Mark the prefix length of each candidate route.
  2. Is there a single longest prefix? If YES → that route wins. STOP.
  3. If prefix lengths are tied → compare AD. Lowest wins.
  4. If AD also tied → compare metric. Lowest wins (or ECMP if tied).

Before every forwarding-decision question, scribble 'P → A → M' in the margin. This three-letter cue forces you into the correct order and catches the trap 100% of the time. Students who build this habit in practice exams rarely lose points to this trap on the real exam.

Second-tier trap: Mixing up AD values. Remember: eBGP is 20 (very low, easy to miss) — it actually beats both EIGRP and OSPF. This sometimes shows up in 'OSPF vs BGP' questions where students assume OSPF wins because they forgot eBGP is 20.

hack.memory: The trap: 'Connected AD 0 always wins.' The truth: prefix length is STEP 1. A /32 from the worst protocol (RIP) beats a /24 from the best source (Connected). Always write 'P → A → M' next to every forwarding question BEFORE choosing an answer. Three letters = no trap can catch you.
hack.practice: Find 15 forwarding-decision questions from Boson ExSim, Pearson, and Anki decks. For each, write 'P → A → M' first. Apply each step in order. Don't peek at the answer until you've worked through all three steps. Compare your answer to the key. Aim for 15/15 — once you're perfect, you own this entire topic on the real exam.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 15 explicitly warns about this trap. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 16 has a section titled 'Determining the Best Route' that highlights the order. Boson practice exams are the best source of trap questions — do at least 3 full practice exams with PAM discipline before the real test. Expect 2-4 trap questions on the actual exam.
